story:
I’m tired of being called his monster. Call me Frank. Yes, I’m stitched, mismatched, oddly colored. But aren’t we all assembled from our pasts? Your scars are inside; mine are just loud. I don’t want to be chased from bars by torches. I want a drink, a chance to be human—or at least be left alone. If I’d been made now, I’d be sandblasted, injected, buffed until I blended in with all the silicone souls. But I don’t mind my look. It keeps me humble.

Twice a week I go to MMMA—Misunderstood Monsters Meeting Anonymous. Phantom, hunchback, Kong, a thirsty count, a lagoon guy—we’re all there, not the nightmares you make us. We meet in a giant auditorium, just in case Kong shows. Anonymity’s rough. You pass a fellow monster at the drugstore buying condoms, and you nod without letting on you overheard the curse, the back pain, or the lack of a head. The Headless Horseman’s pumpkin does the talking; suddenly my problems feel small.

Landing a normal job? Brutal. If you’re not doing movies or theme parks, doors slam. Nanny? No. Customer service? Please. I bounced for a while, until Igor tried to get in. He didn’t fit the vibe, got salty, and threatened to reveal the provenance of certain private parts. That secret stays sewn shut. Meanwhile, the mummy’s an influencer, the Invisible Man does some light robbery, the wolfman shills shaving cream under a full moon, Nessy lifeguards, the shrinking man handles pest control. I found a spot in a beauty store. People compliment my makeup. Monster groupies drift through—into my bolts, curious about my story—but they usually ghost. I figured I’d live quietly, maybe swipe on Mindr, the monster dating app.

Then she walked into MMMA. Hair piled high like a storm cloud, stitching like constellations, eyes you get lost in—one blue, one green. Elsa. Tired of being the eternal scream queen. She said it like a vow, and the room rumbled—Kong grunted, the jack-o’-lantern grinned—but she only had eyes for me. We kept stealing glances while the others vented: a ghost annoyed no one sees him, so he throws things; a cyclops grousing about needing reading glasses.

At the break she asked me to coffee. We hit the all-night diner where the nighthawks perch. She thought she’d seen me at a favorite haunt. I confessed I have a thing for brides in wedding dresses. We traded hopes and dreams over mugs while the werewolf behind us talked razor burn with a witch who had a salve. Her foot brushed mine. Our fingers touched when we reached for sugar. I’ve got a sweet tooth. Heck, I’ve got all of them.

I know what people mean when they say “Frankenstein,” and I try not to snap. Correct me if you must, but spare me the lecture. We’re not worse than you lot; most days we’re just trying to make rent and be kind. She said, “Want to take this to my lab, Mr. Monster?” I said, “Yeah, baby. And for the record, you can call me Frank.”
